CT Traditional

New Hire Resources

CT Traditional Handbook

Expectations

Download PDF

From leadership

We are here to coach, teach, and develop you. You will receive as much training, daily support, and feedback as you can handle. My job is to show you the proven processes we use to make you more money than you ever thought possible. Your job is to be proactive in constantly learning and refining the processes with integrity. Your days will be highly structured to facilitate your success. Your feedback to leadership is crucial and welcome.

What your week looks like

“On call” shifts are when you’re available to accept company leads. Every month you submit your availability and the days you need off. That’s the backbone of your work week.

Standard

Standard is a minimum of 12 “on-call” shifts per week, which includes most Saturdays. Sunday shifts are available on a volunteer basis.

How on-call shifts are structured

ShiftMon–Fri firstMon–Fri lastSat/Sun firstSat/Sun last
Shift 110:00 AM2:00 PM9:00 AM1:00 PM
Shift 21:00 PM5:00 PM1:00 PM4:00 PM
Shift 34:00 PM8:00 PM——

Saturday shifts are 3 per month. Sunday is volunteer only.

Sample week structure

DayTimeWhat
MondayShifts 1, 2, 3On-call
Tuesday9:30–11:30 AMMandatory live team meeting. Then shifts 2 and 3.
WednesdayAll daySpend shift 1 in office on lead generation. Shifts 2 and 3 on-call.
Thursday8:30–9:30 AMCoffee with Closers (mandatory online meeting). Then shifts 1, 2, 3.
FridayShifts 1, 2, 3On-call
Saturday8:30–9:30 AMMiracle Morning (mandatory online meeting). Then shifts 2 and 3.
SundayVolunteerOptional

Goals

Daily10 live solar conversations. Stay connected with the team through the thread and calls.
Weekly2 agreements signed. 2 self-generated leads booked and run.
Monthly6 agreements inclusive of 2 self-generated. 30% close rate.

Communication

We communicate heavily through the team text chain. It’s the primary form of communication. Daily participation is expected. The goal is to stay connected and constantly be learning. The sharper we keep our skills, the more money we make. The text thread is key in that.

Rules of the road

  • Act with integrity. Constantly.
  • Take lots of notes. Constantly.
  • Ask for help. Constantly.
  • This is a full-time job.

The path

You can reasonably expect 10 company leads a month. Three of them will be garbage. Out of the other 7, you’ll be expected to close 50%.

You’ll also bring in 2 self-generated leads a week. We will show you how, and give you the tools and training to make it possible.

That’s 18 leads a month. You’ll be expected to close at 30%, or a minimum of 6 deals a month across all products (solar, roof, battery). Out of those 6 deals, reasonably assume at least 4 hit the roof.

The math

The average deal across all products pays a $3,000 commission to you.

That’s $12,000 a month. $36,000 a quarter. $144,000 a year.

We will help you close at least 3 deals in your first 3 weeks, then ramp up from there.

Launching

Week 1Mon–Fri in NJ. Saturday in CT shadowing a leader all day.
Week 2AM: classrooms in CT. PM: you shadowing leaders, leaders shadowing you on initial self-generated appointments.
Weeks 3–4You running your own leads, shadowed by leaders.
Week 5Goal: 3 agreements signed before end of week 5, so you’re well launched and earning income.
Five-Day Outline

CT Training Week

Download PDF

Mornings in the classroom. Afternoons in shadows. This is the skeleton of the week, expect it to flex based on where each trainee is.

Day 1: Admin and key concepts

Onboarding day.

  • iPads, Salesforce, commissions, help desk, LightReach, Sunlight, One Button, Training Page: all accounts set up and working
  • Activate DOT digital cards, save contact info, verify accounts
  • Shared apps installed (Site Capture, others as assigned)
  • Key concepts script, first pass

Day 2: Script mastery and capture

Nick leads in the morning, I lead in the afternoon.

  • Admin review, loose ends from Day 1
  • Nick: One Draw walkthrough
  • Nick: Site Capture walkthrough
  • Key concepts script, assuming the close, one-touch closing

Day 3: Why, story, and systems

Mike leads on finding your why and sharing your story.

  • Mike: Finding your why, sharing your story
  • Key concepts review, assuming the close, one-touch closing
  • One Draw and Site Capture review
  • Salesforce and One Button walkthrough

Day 4: Pay, listening, and role play

Wyant on pay. Me on active listening and go-backs.

  • Wyant: How you get paid
  • Active listening, following questions, setting go-backs
  • Role play all day, tying it together
  • How to close out a sale with the software and the in-house checklist

Day 5: Put it all together

Full day of role play and wrap-up.

  • Role play all day
  • Close out a sale end-to-end using the software
  • Run the in-house checklist

How to use this week

This outline is the frame, not the script. If a trainee needs more time on concepts before moving to role play, we slow down. If they’re ahead, we ramp earlier into live shadows.

Before You Start

Day Zero Training

Download PDF

Welcome

Homework

While you are in NJ, you have homework from me.

Memorize the key concepts below. These are the basis for everything you will do here.

When you come home, I will hold you accountable for having these concepts memorized word for word. Trust me, doing so will ramp up your commissions fast.

Your list

Make a list of everyone you know who will help you. When you come home from NJ, you’ll be asked to lean on them to practice live. Ask each for 45 minutes, sitting with their most recent electric bill. The goal is simply to learn how to talk to people about solar.

Key concepts

1. Agenda

“My job is to explain a program you’re already paying into. I’m not here to sell you anything. Every electric bill you pay with your hard-earned money, you’re paying for other homeowners to have solar whether you have it or not. The hardest thing with this program is your home needs to qualify. That’s what we’re doing here today.”

“By the end of our conversation, I’ll be able to show you exactly what you’re paying today for electricity vs. what that looks like with solar. If the numbers make sense, I’m happy to help you enroll. If not, that’s totally ok.”

(Spoiler alert: the numbers always make sense.)

2. Qualify

To qualify the home, we prove four things to the utility, the state, and the town:

  1. You need 800 hours of usable sunlight.
  2. The site is good for solar: roof in good condition, structure can handle panel weight plus snow load.
  3. The electrical system is or will be brought up to code.
  4. From day one, it must be less expensive and more rate-stabilized than what they’re doing today.

If we can’t check all those boxes, they don’t qualify and we can’t help them. The good news: if they do qualify, we install the system at no cost. Panels, equipment, permits, applications, literally no money out of pocket.

3. Net metering

All the power from the roof is theirs, registered to their house and meter. Any month the panels produce more than they consume, they carry a negative balance with the utility that rolls forward without penalty.

In Connecticut, all solar projects contribute $0.04 per kWh produced, shown on the bill like a connection fee.

The solar panels produce electricity below wholesale. The electric company buys excess at full retail. As what they charge the homeowner rises each year, so does what they pay back.

No one gets rich off this money. It’s value-add. They get paid for the electricity they produce but don’t use. March through October the system typically produces all their power plus extra, reducing supply and delivery fees.

4. Why it’s not too good to be true

The system of getting electricity to our homes is broken. We lose half our power through delivery, and that’s a third of our carbon footprint.

The biggest grid problem is peak power. Last year utilities paid $1–$4 per kWh of peak power. That number goes up every year.

The state offers this program, and electric companies work with us, because rooftop solar directly combats peak cost. Peak plants are old, inefficient, expensive, and dirty, sitting idle waiting for demand. If we produce that power locally, everybody wins: the homeowner, Trinity, the utility, and the environment.

5. Chat-refined version

“My job is pretty simple, to explain a program you’re already paying into.”

“Every utility customer helps fund solar, whether you have panels or not. What I do is check whether you actually qualify to use it.”

“To qualify, we prove a few things to the utility and the state: your home gets enough usable sunlight (about 800 hours a year), the roof is in good condition and can handle the panels and snow load, the electrical is up to code or can be brought up, and most importantly, that you’ll lower your costs and stabilize your rates from day one.”

“If I can’t check all those boxes, you wouldn’t qualify and I won’t move forward. The good news is if you do qualify, the system is installed with no money out of pocket. Panels, equipment, permits, applications, everything.”

“Typically the power from the panels comes in about 30–40% less than what you’re paying the utility now. If the numbers make sense, I’m happy to help you enroll today. If not, that’s totally ok.”

North star

Don’t sell solar. Sell an option.

Trainer Guide

Day 1: Admin and Setup

Download PDF

Day 1 is the admin foundation. Everything downstream assumes these accounts work, these passwords are remembered, and these apps are installed. Walk through them with your trainee, don’t just send the links.

Tour the office

Walk the floor. Point out who does what. Introduce the trainee to every face they’ll need to know.

Device setup

  1. Rename each iPad. Settings › General › About › Name. Format: Shady’s iPad. This is so AirDrop works cleanly when we share materials.
  2. Order a Flexbook Touch Case. 7-inch wireless keyboard case with trackpad. Order these while trainees are in NJ and have the case on when they return.
  3. DOT digital cards. Activate with banner and photo using the QR code. Save all contact info and verify the account.
  4. Passwords. You must remember these. Write them somewhere secure before you leave the office today.
  5. Browser and AirDrop. Change default browser to Safari (Settings › Apps). Set Start Page to 3 favorites, delete the default favorites. Turn AirDrop to Everyone for 10 minutes (Settings › General › AirDrop).
  6. Messages. iPad: Settings › Apps › Messages › create new profile with Trinity email. iPhone: set up Messages with personal phone number.

Essential links

Salesforce uses the Microsoft password issued in NJ.

Salesforce (SSO)trinity-solar.lightning.force.com
Commissions (OnePay)onepay.trinity-solar.com
Help Deskhelpdesk.trinitysolarsystems.com/support/home
LightReach / Palmettoauth.palmetto.com/login
Sunlight Financialslfportal.com/orange
One Buttononebutton.trinity-solar.com/#/admin
CT Resource Pagect-resource-page.com

Shared apps

  • Site Capture, primary field tool
  • Doc Scan (optional). I don’t use it. Teach them to take proper notes in Salesforce and save documents in Box instead.

Get them on the lists

Add the trainee to each of these so they start seeing traffic from day one:

  • Team text chain
  • MM (morning meeting) list
  • CWC (coffee with closers) list
  • Email chain

Roofing reference

IKO Dynasty Homeowner BrochureSharePoint › Roofing › IKO Dynasty
Cambridge Roof referenceSharePoint › Roofing › Cambridge

Homework

  • Continue memorizing the key concepts script
  • Set up Site Capture
  • Walk through the Watrous One-Touch Close Worksheet

Reminder

If the homeowner has a supplier that isn’t the default utility, the supply portion of the bill may look different. Always pull the actual PDF of the most recent bill before quoting, never rely on a verbal summary.

Clean-Submit Process

2026 Paperwork to Submit a Deal

Download PDF

This is the process that gets you from quoting the job to submitting the deal cleanly. A deal submitted clean means you get paid in weeks instead of months. Do every step with the homeowner.

Quick list

Move to Contract • Credit Status • Sign Agreements • Activate Account • Salesforce Sync • Trinity Documents • Energy Audit Form • Profit Sharing / W9 • Save each other in your phones • Box • Salesforce • Site Capture

1. Move to Contract

Work through One Button to reach the quote page. Click “Next” to move the deal into contract status.

2. Credit Status

If this is a cash deal, skip to step 5.

Click the credit icon. It walks you through running credit. When credit passes, you will receive an email. The homeowner will receive an email whether they pass or fail.

3. Sign Agreements (LightReach or non-cash)

If this is a cash deal, skip to step 5.

  1. Wait for the credit icon to change (don’t proceed if credit didn’t pass).
  2. Click the blue Submit Quote button. Only after credit has passed.
  3. A pen icon appears under Sign Finance Partner Contract. Click it, choose In Person, and the agreement opens.
  4. Read through the agreement with the homeowner. Have them initial everywhere prompted.
  5. The agreement is emailed to you. Open that email and countersign it.

4. Activate Account (LightReach only)

Done on the homeowner’s phone. They’ll have several emails from LightReach or Palmetto. Open the one that says Activate Account. They’ll answer follow-up questions, validate identity, and add a bank account to their profile.

5. Salesforce Sync

Click the sync icon. This links Salesforce with any finance partners and auto-populates the forms for Trinity Documents. Wait a moment. When it completes, you’ll see the sync confirmation screen.

6. Create Trinity Documents

Click the Trinity Documents button. If step 5 wasn’t done correctly, you’ll see an error. If step 5 was done right, you’ll see the document list. This creates the combo pack and any cash contracts.

How to explain the combo pack

“Nothing in this series of documents is a contract. These are forms that say some version of this:

you are the homeowner, you are on the electric bill, we are the installer, this bank owns the system. These are documents that will go to the state, the city, and the electric company, basically you agreeing to let us act as your agent.”

Two signing options:

  • In-person signing. Read through everything with the homeowner and have them sign on your device. You countersign after.
  • Send for signature. The docs come to you first, then auto-send to the homeowner after you sign.

7. Energy Audit Form

Mandatory. The State of CT requires it to approve the solar application. The audit itself is performed by the state’s vendor (currently CMC), not by us.

Filling out this form triggers a call from the audit company to schedule with the homeowner. The audit company answers all questions during that call.

What to tell the homeowner

There is no cost and no obligation to buy anything. But the audit must be scheduled, or the state will not approve our application for the job.

Use: docs.google.com/forms/...viewform. If the homeowner is tech-savvy, bring it up on your device and fill out together. If not, text it for them to complete.

8. Profit Sharing and W9

Review the program (again):

Roof referral$500
First solar referral$1,000
Each additional solar referral$2,000

How to ask

“The best practice is for you to start a group text between me and your friend. I’ll take it from there. I’m very low-key.”

W9: “In order for our company to pay you, we need to have a W9 on file.” Use trinitysolar.com/documentupload. Fill out and submit.

9. Save each other in your phones

Take a picture of you with the homeowner. Explain that you save it to their contact in your phone. Make it fun. Save their info (email, physical address, everything) and mark them as a Trinity Customer.

10. Box: your virtual filing cabinet

Take screenshots and save three items into the Sales Docs folder, labeled exactly:

  • a: design (design values must be visible)
  • b: bill (all pages, crystal clear)
  • c: ppa (one sheet)

11. Salesforce: the submit

Crucial. Doing this correctly submits the job to sales ops and gets everything moving. Getting you paid in weeks not months.

  1. On the Opportunity page, hit Edit.
  2. Click First Appointment Completed.
  3. Under Opportunity Progress Details: Stage 4 › Contracted. Stage Status › Signed.
  4. Key Dates and Milestones: fill in Contract Signed Date.
  5. Salesperson Notes: initials + today’s date + detailed notes. Update this every time something happens during the process.
  6. Hit Save. If something’s wrong, you’ll be prompted to fix it.
  7. Scroll to Calendar Events (right side). Click New Solar Install. Click ahead ~45 days. Choose a date not already filled with installs. Record Type › Solar Install › Create Event.
  8. Back on the Opportunity page, wait for the calendar to process. You should get a text confirmation.
  9. Click Buttons at the top. Choose Submit for Contract Review. Wait for the confirmation pop-up. Click Details. The Contract Submitted button turns green with a checkmark.

12. Site Capture

Done on your phone or device. The app walks you through every step. Practice this with a mentor before you run it live.

13. Skedulo

Before you leave the home, close the job out in Skedulo. In Outcome Notes, leave your initials, the date, and a brief description of the sit.

Referrals, Reviews, and Relationships

Install Day Checklist

Download PDF

Referrals first

Referrals start early in your process. Talk about the program. Reference it often: “Remind me to tell you about our referral program.” Get the homeowner thinking about it. If you don’t, corporate will start working the neighborhood and you won’t get credit. I want to earn money for you.

The day before and day of

  • Call customer the night before: “Are you going to be home? I’ll be there at ___”
  • Bring food for the crew. Big Y party sub is $20, or party pizza and Gatorade. Thank the crew.
  • Give your cards to the crew in case neighbors stop by when you leave.
  • Bring a gift for the homeowner, hopefully something personalized they would like ($20–$30).
  • Check in with the homeowner. See how it’s going. Make sure they’re happy.

Ask while you’re there

  • Do you know names of neighbors? Would you mind introducing me?
  • Would you mind giving us a 5-star Google review? Mention me by name.
  • Would you mind giving us a BBB review? Names can’t be put on BBB.
  • Would you post a Facebook testimonial? Tag me in the post so I can see the comments.

Knock the box

Apologize for the noise. Conduct a short survey with the neighbor.

Script

Please let me know if the music is too loud, crude, or inappropriate. If we clean properly. If there is too much traffic. Give cards. “Just so you know, they installed panels at no cost if you qualify.” The box is your business. Get referrals.

Referral sheet

This is the best part of the program. Be specific when you ask. “Who do you know that is a...”

  • Homeowner
  • Co-worker
  • Person that owns their home
  • Electric vehicle owner
  • Recently moved in
  • Has a new roof or needs one
  • Has a swimming pool
  • Complains about electric costs
  • On fixed income
  • Mother, father, brother, sister, cousins

I need your help. Who do you know that I can help? Give them time to fill out the list.

Field Guide

Community Event Instructions

Download PDF

Purpose

Helping our partners raise funds

You are helping our partner organization raise funds through the Trinity Solar partnership. Every lead must be entered through the partnership portal so the organization receives credit.

What to say

Opening script

“We’re helping raise funds for the [Organization]. When you have a free, no-obligation solar consultation, the organization receives $100. If you install solar, $1,000 is donated. This program has already generated over $840,000 and we can’t wait until it’s over a million.”

Every lead (must be legible)

Collect the following for each homeowner:

  • Full name
  • Street address
  • Phone number
  • Email address

Helpful notes if time allows: roof condition, trees or shading, battery interest, best time to call or meet.

What to tell the homeowner

“Someone will call you in the next few days to schedule your home evaluation, so please answer the call.”

Important

  • All leads must be entered into the partnership portal, or the organization receives no credit.
  • Take photos of all sign-up sheets after the event and send to Lori Scala at (203) 895-8968.
  • Our team will pre-qualify leads, schedule consultations, and add them to your calendar.

Referrals that support the organization

If someone is connected to or wants to support the organization:

  1. Enter the lead through the partnership portal.
  2. Add “Referred by ___” in the notes.
  3. If they install solar, collect a W9 from the referring homeowner.

This ensures all partners feel supported and no leads are taken away.

Refer-a-Friend payouts

TriggerTo organizationTo referring homeowner
First referral installs solar$500$500
Each additional referral install$1,000$1,000
Appointment generated (no install)$100—

After the event

  • Send all leads to Lori as soon as possible.
  • Leads must be entered within 24 hours.

Questions or support

Lori Scala(203) 895-8968
